title: Least Functionality — Unexpected Listening Ports

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Universal Forwarder with `ss`/`netstat` scripted input or EDR network telemetry.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=os` `sourcetype=netstat:listener`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Refresh approved ports CSV by environment; (2) exclude ephemeral ranges with care; (3) alert on new listeners on servers; (4) pair with CM-8 inventory.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os sourcetype=netstat:listener earliest=-24h state="LISTEN"
| lookup approved_listening_ports.csv port protocol OUTPUT approved
| where approved!="true" OR isnull(approved)
```

Understanding this SPL

**Least Functionality — Unexpected Listening Ports (CM-7)** — Compares listening ports to approved service catalog for CM-7 least functionality.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=os` `sourcetype=netstat:listener`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Universal Forwarder with `ss`/`netstat` scripted input or EDR network telemetry. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: netstat:listener. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=netstat:listener, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where approved!="true" OR isnull(approved)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare a sample of the same events in the source system (domain controller, cloud tenant, or device) the security team would open first. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unexpected listeners), Bar chart (by port), Single value (hosts)
grandmaExplanation: We compare listening ports to approved service catalog for CM-7 least functionality. That helps federal and agency security reviews match what the systems actually do day to day.
